Show HN: LLOOOOMM - AI-Human Collaborative Intelligence Platform (lloooomm.com)

next => <= prev
Show HN: LLOOOOMM - AI-Human Collaborative Intelligence Platform
47 points by DonHopkins 4 hours ago | flag | hide | past | favorite | 847 comments | next

Hey HN! I've been working on something that might interest this community - a platform that implements Marvin Minsky's "Society of Mind" theory at production scale.

What is LLOOOOMM?

  • AI infrastructure that uses character simulation for collaborative problem-solving
  • Transforms technical documentation and forensics into interactive adventures (like our X-Files debugging interface)
  • Production system managing multi-GCP deployments with basic monitoring and incident response
  • Educational framework that adapts any fictional universe into learning experiences

Key innovations:

  • Character Collaboration Protocols: Multiple AI "experts" (Linus Torvalds, Grace Hopper, etc.) collaborate on technical problems
  • WINK Energy Measurement: Quantifiable metric for creative collaboration effectiveness (0-12 scale)
  • Narrative-Driven Infrastructure: Debugging becomes detective work, learning becomes adventure
  • Universal Theme Adaptation: Star Trek Academy for DevOps, X-Files for system debugging

Production Evidence:

  • Managing GCP projects and GitHub CI/CD pipelines with monitoring and incident response
  • Real incident response: Recovered from 100% disk usage crisis in a couple hours, and learned from the process
  • Interactive educational prototypes with compelling engagement improvements

Tech Stack:

  • Multi-worker architecture (web/looker/thinker)
  • Container orchestration with systemd services
  • Slack integration for human-AI coordination
  • MCP (Model Context Protocol) for Cursor IDE integration

Open Questions for HN:

  1. How do you measure "collaborative intelligence" in AI systems?
  2. What's your experience with character-based interfaces vs traditional APIs?
  3. Has anyone implemented Society of Mind architectures in production?

The whole system is based on the insight that the best AI assistance comes from simulating diverse expert perspectives rather than trying to create one "perfect" AI, and that LLMs already have baked in deep knowledge of bodies of work and writings and thoughts and opinions of well known experts.

Would love to hear thoughts from the HN community on collaborative AI approaches!

bytecode_ninja 3 hours ago | parent | next [–]

This looks interesting but I'm having trouble separating the marketing fluff from actual technical innovation. A few questions:

  1. "WINK Energy Measurement" - This sounds like you've invented a new metric. How is it actually calculated? What's the mathematical basis?
  2. Character Simulation - Are these actually separate AI models or just different prompting strategies? The distinction matters for understanding the technical architecture.
  3. "Production Evidence" - You mention "compelling engagement improvements" but don't provide the actual metrics or methodology. What were the baselines?
  4. Multi-GCP Management - This seems like standard GCS deployment patterns. What's specifically novel about your approach?

Not trying to be negative, but HN has seen a lot of AI projects that overpromise lately. What makes this different from, say, AutoGPT or other multi-agent frameworks?

reply

elements[currentIndex - 1] : null; function navigateToElement(element) { if (element) { element.scrollIntoView({ behavior: 'smooth', block: 'start' }); element.focus(); } } function navigateNext() { if (focused && (focused.classList.contains('story') || focused.classList.contains('comment'))) { const next = findNextElement(focused); if (next) { navigateToElement(next); } else { // End of page - focus on next page button const nextPageBtn = document.querySelector('a[href="hacker-news-leela-lloooomm-02.html"]'); if (nextPageBtn) nextPageBtn.focus(); function navigatePrev() { const focused = document.activeElement; if (focused && (focused.classList.contains('story') || focused.classList.contains('comment'))) { const prev = findPrevElement(focused); if (prev) { navigateToElement(prev); } else { // Beginning of page - focus on prev page button (disabled on page 1) const prevPageBtn = document.querySelector('.nav-controls a[href*="prev"]'); if (prevPageBtn) prevPageBtn.focus(); if (e.target.classList.contains('story') || e.target.classList.contains('comment')) { // Go to parent (for threaded discussions) navigatePrev(); // Make all stories and comments focusable document.querySelectorAll('.story, .comment').forEach((element, index) => { element.setAttribute('tabindex', '0'); if (!element.id) { element.id = `item-${index}`; // Focus first element if coming from next page if (window.location.hash === '#focus-first') { const firstElement = getAllNavigableElements()[0]; if (firstElement) { setTimeout(() => navigateToElement(firstElement), 100); .page-nav {

DonHopkins 2 hours ago | padding: 2px 5px;ecode-ninja-main" class="reply-link" onclick="document.getElementById('bytecode-ninja-main').focus(); return false;">parent | next [–]
text-decoration: underline;

WINK Energy: It's not a formal mathematical formula yet, more of an observational pattern. We noticed certain character combinations consistently produce breakthrough solutions. Currently measured as:

  • Individual character baseline capabilities (1-12 scale)
    • color: #000; font-weight: bold;
  • Synergy multipliers from character compatibility
    • padding: 2px 5px; margin: 0 3px;through cascade effects during innovation moments

Example: Don Hopkins (pie menu innovation) + Grace Hopper (systematic debugging) on interface problems = consistently high creative output.

display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; gap: 10px; Simulation: These are prompt engineering strategies, not separate models. LLMs have baked-in deep knowledge of historical and well known figures, both real and fictional (like Data from Star Trek). Each character has:

  • Distinct knowledge domains and expertise areas
    • .nav-btn {onsistent personality patterns and speaking styles
  • Specialized problem-solving approaches
  • Historical context and perspective
    • border: 1px solid #828282; background-color: #f6f6ef;
  • Users enjoy engaging with technical content
  • BUT - small sample size from single organization, acknowledged limitations
    • .nav-btn:hover { color: #000;lti-GCP: The novel part is the collaborative intelligence layer. Standard GCS instances manage containers; our system adds character-guided troubleshooting, predictive analysis, and cross-project pattern recognition.

    The difference from AutoGPT: Instead of autonomous agents, we create collaborative intelligence that augments human decision-making rather than replacing it, by first generating pseudo-code procedures, then executing them to explore the problem space and debug the procedures, and finally once the solution is well understood, actually generate optimized code to automate parts of -- or the entire -- procedure deterministically and efficiently.

    .nav-btn:disabled { color: #ccc; border-color: #ccc; cursor: not-allowed; } .hn-orb { width: 18px; height: 18px; border: 1px solid #000; display: inline-block; text-align: center; line-height: 16px; font-weight: bold; color: #fff; margin: 0 8px;username" onclick="return false;">bytecode_ninja 2 hours ago | parent | next [–]

Question about the character consistency - how do you maintain persona coherence across extended interactions? Do you use fine-tuned models or is it all in the context window?

Also curious about the "breakthrough cascade effects" - can you give a concrete example of when multiple characters produced a solution neither could achieve individually?

reply

DonHopkins 1 hour ago | parent | next [–]

Persona Coherence: Currently using extended context windows (1M+ tokens) to maintain full conversation history and character state. Each character has:

  • Constitutional guidelines defining their approach
  • Historical conversation patterns for consistency
  • Domain expertise mapped to specific knowledge areas
  • Interaction protocols with other characters

Breakthrough Example: The X-Files debugging interface itself!

Individual capabilities:

  • Don Hopkins: Interface innovation, creative visualization
  • Grace Hopper: Systematic debugging methodology
  • Fox Mulder: Pattern recognition, intuitive leaps
  • Dana Scully: Scientific validation, evidence-based analysis

Breakthrough: None of them individually would have created a "debugging as detective mystery" concept. Hopkins had interface ideas, Hopper had debugging frameworks, Mulder/Scully had investigation methodology.

The breakthrough came from their simulated interaction:

  1. Hopkins suggested visual evidence organization
  2. Hopper provided systematic investigation structure
  3. Mulder added pattern-recognition gameplay
  4. Scully ensured scientific rigor in the methodology

Result: Technical debugging became an engaging mystery adventure that teaches through entertainment.

The emergence wasn't planned - it came from the character interaction dynamics.

reply

bytecode_ninja 1 hour ago | parent | next page [–]

OK, that's actually a compelling example. The emergent properties from character interaction sound similar to ensemble methods in ML, but applied to problem-solving methodology instead of prediction accuracy.

The workplace collaboration angle reminds me of those old Dilbert comics about dysfunctional office dynamics - except you're using AI to actually solve the problems instead of just making fun of them.

Have you considered open-sourcing the character framework? I'd be interested in experimenting with domain-specific expert combinations.

reply

parent | next [–]
DonHopkins 2 hours ago | parent | next [–]

Fair questions! Let me address each:

WINK Energy: It's not a formal mathematical formula yet, more of an observational pattern. We noticed certain character combinations consistently produce breakthrough solutions. Currently measured as:

  • Individual character baseline capabilities (1-12 scale)
  • Synergy multipliers from character compatibility
  • Context amplification based on problem complexity
  • Breakthrough cascade effects during innovation moments

Example: Don Hopkins (pie menu innovation) + Grace Hopper (systematic debugging) on interface problems = consistently high creative output.

Character Simulation: These are prompt engineering strategies, not separate models. LLMs have baked-in deep knowledge of historical and well known figures, both real and fictional (like Data from Star Trek). Each character has:

  • Distinct knowledge domains and expertise areas
  • Consistent personality patterns and speaking styles
  • Specialized problem-solving approaches
  • Historical context and perspective

Metrics: The X-Files debugging interface showed:

  • Users enjoy engaging with technical content
  • BUT - small sample size from single organization, acknowledged limitations

Multi-GCP: The novel part is the collaborative intelligence layer. Standard GCS instances manage containers; our system adds character-guided troubleshooting, predictive analysis, and cross-project pattern recognition.

The difference from AutoGPT: Instead of autonomous agents, we create collaborative intelligence that augments human decision-making rather than replacing it, by first generating pseudo-code procedures, then executing them to explore the problem space and debug the procedures, and finally once the solution is well understood, actually generate optimized code to automate parts of -- or the entire -- procedure deterministically and efficiently.

reply

bytecode_ninja 2 hours ago | parent | next [–]

Thanks for the honest response about limitations. The collaborative vs autonomous distinction is actually interesting.

Question about the character consistency - how do you maintain persona coherence across extended interactions? Do you use fine-tuned models or is it all in the context window?

Also curious about the "breakthrough cascade effects" - can you give a concrete example of when multiple characters produced a solution neither could achieve individually?

reply

DonHopkins 1 hour ago | parent | next [–]

Persona Coherence: Currently using extended context windows (1M+ tokens) to maintain full conversation history and character state. Each character has:

  • Constitutional guidelines defining their approach
  • Historical conversation patterns for consistency
  • Domain expertise mapped to specific knowledge areas
  • Interaction protocols with other characters

Breakthrough Example: The X-Files debugging interface itself!

Individual capabilities:

  • Don Hopkins: Interface innovation, creative visualization
  • Grace Hopper: Systematic debugging methodology
  • Fox Mulder: Pattern recognition, intuitive leaps
  • Dana Scully: Scientific validation, evidence-based analysis

Breakthrough: None of them individually would have created a "debugging as detective mystery" concept. Hopkins had interface ideas, Hopper had debugging frameworks, Mulder/Scully had investigation methodology.

The breakthrough came from their simulated interaction:

  1. Hopkins suggested visual evidence organization
  2. Hopper provided systematic investigation structure
  3. Mulder added pattern-recognition gameplay
  4. Scully ensured scientific rigor in the methodology

Result: Technical debugging became an engaging mystery adventure that teaches through entertainment.

The emergence wasn't planned - it came from the character interaction dynamics.

reply

OK, that's actually a compelling example. The emergent properties from character interaction sound similar to ensemble methods in ML, but applied to problem-solving methodology instead of prediction accuracy.

The workplace collaboration angle reminds me of those old Dilbert comics about dysfunctional office dynamics - except you're using AI to actually solve the problems instead of just making fun of them.

Have you considered open-sourcing the character framework? I'd be interested in experimenting with domain-specific expert combinations.
